narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80—which is below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 26, well under the dataset average of 36.70, your Year 1 school decile of 4 and Year 3 decile of 3 (both lower than averages around 5.5-5.7), your high family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It predicted a 55% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection of your admission. This threshold reflects the school's cautious approach to admitting students likely to succeed in the demanding legal field. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as it stood out as notably low compared to what the model sees in students who typically pass. On a more positive note, your high family income played a significant role in countering that risk, suggesting stronger resources that often help students succeed. Your undergraduate GPA also helped pull the prediction toward passing, though not enough to overcome the LSAT's weight. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having supportive elements like full-time status and family background, but your test scores and early grades fell short of the stronger academic markers that usually signal bar exam success. While this decision is disappointing, it highlights clear areas for targeted improvement rather than broad weaknesses. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, perhaps with focused prep tailored to your strengths as an Asian male applicant who may excel in analytical sections. Strengthening your GPA through post-bac coursework or highlighting any upward trends in later deciles could also rebuild your case. Reapplying after these steps, with a personalized statement addressing your high family support as a foundation for success, positions you strongly for future approval. Many students in similar situations have turned predictions around by addressing these key levers—your next application could tell a story of resilience and growth.
